name: Black cabbage / cavolo nero (Tuscan/Lacinato kale, Brassica oleracea var. acephala) (food medium)

components.name: water
components.evidence: Moisture 89.6 g/100 g; defines the aqueous phase of the medium.

components.name: D-glucose
components.evidence: Glucose 0.38 g/100 g fresh. Kale is a low-sugar leafy brassica; free monosaccharides dominate over sucrose.

components.name: D-fructose
components.source: USDA FDC 323505; Ayaz et al. 2006 Food Chem 96:572-579 (fructose predominant, 2011 mg/100g dw)
components.evidence: Fructose 0.43 g/100 g fresh; both USDA and Ayaz find fructose > glucose > sucrose in kale/black cabbage.

components.name: dietary fiber
components.evidence: Total dietary fiber 4.1 g/100 g (cellulose/hemicellulose/pectin); largely non-fermentable for most single-strain models but a carbon reservoir.

components.name: citric acid
components.source: Ayaz et al. 2006 Food Chem 96:572-579 (2213 mg/100g dry wt, black cabbage) converted to fresh wt via 10.4% dry matter (USDA water 89.6%)
components.evidence: MISSING from FooDB record. Citric is the DOMINANT organic acid of kale/black cabbage; major TCA-cycle-feeding carbon source. Fresh-wt estimate; genotype range 1962-3743 mg/100g dw.

components.name: L-malic acid
components.source: Ayaz et al. 2006 (151 mg/100g dry wt) converted to fresh wt via 10.4% dry matter
components.evidence: MISSING from FooDB record. Second organic acid; Ayaz black-cabbage sample low in malic relative to citric (other genotypes report higher malic, 454-3175 mg/100g dw).

components.name: total protein
components.evidence: Protein 2.9 g/100 g; amino acids below are the protein-bound + free profile.

components.name: L-glutamate
components.evidence: 0.33 g/100 g; most abundant amino acid.

components.name: L-phenylalanine
components.evidence: MISSING from FooDB record. 0.15 g/100 g.

components.name: L-serine
components.evidence: 0.12 g/100 g. Replaces the erroneous D-serine in the FooDB record (plants contain the L-enantiomer).

components.name: L-valine
components.evidence: MISSING from FooDB record. 0.16 g/100 g.

components.name: polyunsaturated fatty acids
components.evidence: PUFA 0.67 g/100 g (mainly alpha-linolenic and linoleic acid).

components.name: dodecanoic acid (lauric acid)
components.evidence: Present in FooDB record as a trace fatty acid; retained but unquantified (minor plant leaf lipid).

components.name: iron
components.evidence: Fe 1.6 mg/100 g. Consolidates the three redundant FooDB iron entries (Iron, Iron Fe3+, Fe2+ mitochondria).

components.name: molybdate (molybdenum)
components.evidence: Trace essential micronutrient (nitrate reductase / Mo cofactor); retained unquantified.

components.name: sulfate (sulfur)
components.evidence: Brassicas are sulfur-rich (glucosinolates); sulfate retained as S source, unquantified.

components.name: ammonium
components.evidence: Free ammonium/nitrate present in leaf tissue; retained as inorganic N, unquantified.

components.name: choline
components.evidence: Choline 0.5 mg/100 g (note: USDA free choline; kale total choline is higher in some sources).

components.name: glycine betaine
components.evidence: Genuine brassica osmolyte / methyl donor; retained unquantified.

components.name: retinol activity / vitamin A
components.evidence: Vitamin A 241 ug RAE/100 g (provitamin-A from carotenoids; kale contains no preformed retinol - the FooDB 'Retinol' entry is really provitamin-A activity).

components.name: alpha-carotene
components.evidence: Present in FooDB record; USDA Foundation entry lists 0, but kale genuinely carries minor alpha-carotene. Retained unquantified.

components.name: phytomenadione (vitamin K1)
components.evidence: Vitamin K 390 ug/100 g (kale is among the richest dietary K1 sources).

components.name: quercetin
components.evidence: Genuine kale flavonol (mostly as glycosides); retained. No BiGG metabolite - non-nutritive but real.

remove.name: D-Serine
remove.reason: Wrong enantiomer: plants/kale contain L-serine, not D-serine. Almost certainly a FooDB/BiGG ID-mapping artifact. Replaced by L-serine (0.12 g/100g).

remove.name: Nickel
remove.reason: Non-essential trace element for the food matrix; ultra-trace contaminant, not a defining growth nutrient. FooDB predicted/soil-uptake noise.

remove.name: Co2+
remove.reason: Cobalt is ultra-trace and not a required plant nutrient; retained value is FooDB predicted noise rather than a measured growth-relevant amount. (Cobalt matters only via B12, which kale lacks.)

remove.name: CO2 CO2
remove.reason: Gas-exchange metabolite of the model environment, not a food-derived nutrient of black cabbage. Belongs to the aerobic medium boundary, not the composition.

remove.name: O2 O2
remove.reason: Model boundary gas (aerobic exchange), not a food nutrient. Provided by the medium environment, not the leaf.

remove.name: H+
remove.reason: Proton/charge-balance exchange metabolite, not a food composition nutrient.

remove.name: Iron (Fe3+) / Fe2+ mitochondria
remove.reason: Redundant duplicate iron entries (three FooDB iron rows). Consolidated into a single measured 'iron' 1.6 mg/100g; the compartment-tagged 'Fe2+ mitochondria' is a model artifact.

missing_added: citric acid (dominant organic acid, ~230 mg/100g fresh)
D-fructose (0.43 g/100g) - individual sugars; FooDB had only sucrose
quantitative amounts attached to every mineral, vitamin and amino acid
keep_uncovered: kaempferol (major kale flavonol glycoside; no BiGG metabolite)
quercetin (flavonol; genuine phytochemical, no metabolic BiGG met)
lutein + zeaxanthin (~6261 ug/100g, USDA FDC 323505; dominant kale xanthophylls, no BiGG met)
glucosinolates / glucobrassicin-sinigrin family (defining brassica phytochemicals, no BiGG met)
sources: USDA FoodData Central FDC 323505 (Kale, raw, Foundation Foods) - full fresh-weight panel: proximates, individual sugars, 18 amino acids, fatty acids, minerals, vitamins, carotenoids

Ayaz et al. 2006, Food Chemistry 96:572-579, 'Nutrient contents of kale (Brassica oleraceae L. var. acephala DC.)' - explicitly 'black cabbage'; source for organic acids (citric 2213, malic 151 mg/100g dw) and free-sugar profile (fructose>glucose>sucrose)
FooDB FOOD00247 (original record; provenance for glycine betaine, molybdate, sulfate, chloride, ammonium, quercetin, lauric acid)
Kale review, Cogent Food & Agriculture 2020, doi:10.1080/23311932.2020.1811048 (organic-acid genotype ranges: citric 1962-3743, malic 454-3175 mg/100g dw)
notes: Black cabbage = cavolo nero / Tuscan (Lacinato) kale, Brassica oleracea var. acephala - the exact taxon of Ayaz 2006 ('black cabbage'). It is a LOW-sugar, protein- and mineral-rich leafy brassica: dominant fermentable carbon sources are the free sugars (glucose+fructose+sucrose ~1.0 g/100g), the abundant amino acid pool (Glu, Asp, Leu, Arg lead; total protein 2.9 g/100g), and citric acid (~0.23 g/100g fresh) as the leading organic acid - the latter two were entirely absent from the FooDB record and are the key additions. Organic-acid amounts are fresh-weight estimates from dry-weight literature (10.4% dry matter via USDA water 89.6%), so treat citric/malic as medium confidence; all USDA panel values are high confidence. Kale contains no B12 or preformed retinol (vitamin A is provitamin-A carotenoid activity).
